tagline: A guided sample project with every major artifact: council, report, survey, prototype, session, hypothesis, decision, notes, assets, and a flow.

project.title: Onboarding showcase — TeamPulse rollout
project.goal: Show how Sonaloop turns one research question into connected artifacts a team can inspect, challenge, and hand off.
project.description: TeamPulse is a lightweight workplace pulse-check for hybrid operations teams. This showcase is built for onboarding: it intentionally includes every major inspector artifact at least once, so the product tour can walk a real graph instead of explaining empty navigation.

personas.description: Operations lead at a 120-person logistics company; owns shift handovers, field-team escalations, and weekly improvement meetings.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Dark blazer, warehouse visitor badge, phone always face-up on the table.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Female-read operations lead in her late thirties, pragmatic expression, clean studio illustration.

segment.attitude: will try tools only when they reduce meeting load

role.title: Head of Operations
role.responsibilities: Keeps three sites aligned across handovers, escalation logs, and weekly improvement reviews.
role.decision_power: Can sponsor pilots up to €15k with HR sign-off.

company_context.stack: Teams, Excel, paper shift boards, Jira for central projects
company_context.operating_model: Fast daily standups; formal decisions happen in Friday ops review.

goals: Make employee feedback visible without another meeting
Give site leads one accountable follow-up list
constraints: No new tool can add admin to shift leads
Works council must see privacy defaults early
Mobile usage matters more than dashboards

relationships.name: Tarek
relationships.friction: accepts useful tools but kills anything that slows shift start

personality.communication_style: short, concrete, asks for owners and dates
personality.risk_tolerance: medium when the pilot is reversible
personality.character_notes: Judges software by whether it survives Monday morning.

pain_points: Feedback arrives as anecdotes after problems have already repeated
Dashboards often hide who owns the next action
Site leads distrust anything that looks like surveillance
success_criteria: A site lead can close the loop in under five minutes
The works council has no open privacy objection

personas.description: HR business partner supporting distributed blue-collar teams; accountable for engagement reporting and employee relations.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Soft cardigan, annotated notebook, calm posture.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Female-read HR partner in her thirties, thoughtful, warm professional illustration.

segment.attitude: cares about trust and explainability before metrics

role.title: HR business partner
role.responsibilities: Engagement checks, works-council preparation, manager coaching, and pulse survey interpretation.
role.decision_power: Co-decides pilots with operations and legal.

company_context.stack: Personio, Teams, shared Drive, survey exports
company_context.operating_model: Monthly engagement review, ad hoc escalation calls.

goals: Make survey results actionable for managers
Avoid creating a second reporting process
constraints: Low response teams must not be blamed publicly

relationships.name: Jana
relationships.friction: asks hard questions about anonymity thresholds

personality.communication_style: precise, low-drama, translates risk into policy language
personality.risk_tolerance: low on privacy, medium on reversible process pilots
personality.character_notes: Will block a shiny dashboard if employees cannot understand it.

pain_points: Leaders ask for numbers before agreeing on what they mean
Follow-up promises disappear after town halls
success_criteria: Employees know what happens after they answer
Managers receive a small number of concrete next actions

personas.description: Shift lead at a warehouse site; coordinates ten workers and hates tools that turn into homework after the late shift.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Work jacket, tired eyes, practical watch.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Male-read warehouse shift lead in his forties, direct expression, realistic illustration.

segment.attitude: skeptical until the first shift proves it saves time

role.title: Warehouse shift lead
role.responsibilities: Coordinates daily staffing, break cover, incidents, and handover notes.
role.decision_power: Can make or break adoption through daily usage.

company_context.stack: WhatsApp side-channel, paper board, scanner terminal
company_context.operating_model: Work starts fast; any admin waits until the line is stable.

goals: Stop repeating the same staffing complaint
Keep the team focused during shift start
Avoid being judged by anonymous complaints without context
constraints: No desktop time during active shift
German and Turkish are both used informally on the floor
Trust is lost if answers feel traceable

relationships.name: Nina
relationships.friction: pushes for clean reporting; he pushes for less admin

personality.working_style: practical, protects the team from noise
personality.risk_tolerance: low for tools that expose his team
personality.character_notes: Will adopt anything that makes the next shift less chaotic.

pain_points: Feedback tools arrive as management projects
Late-shift issues are forgotten by morning
success_criteria: He can log and close one issue from his phone
The team sees one visible improvement within a week

personas.description: Finance controller reviewing SaaS spend; wants proof that another engagement tool will change behavior, not just produce a nicer chart.

identity_traits.appearance_notes: Pressed shirt, spreadsheet window always open, compact laptop bag.
identity_traits.avatar_profile: Male-read finance controller in his late forties, measured expression, professional illustration.

segment.attitude: requires evidence before approving recurring spend

role.title: Finance controller
role.responsibilities: Approves SaaS renewals, checks pilot ROI, reports budget variance.
role.decision_power: Can reject recurring spend or force a smaller pilot.

company_context.operating_model: Quarterly cost review; prefers capped pilots with exit criteria.

goals: Avoid yet another unused SaaS subscription
Tie engagement work to measurable operational outcomes
constraints: Needs a stop/go criterion before launch
Will compare against current survey tooling

relationships.name: Mira
relationships.friction: brings trust arguments he accepts only when paired with operating metrics

personality.communication_style: calm, numeric, asks what changes if the answer is bad

pain_points: Engagement tools report activity, not outcomes
Pilot success criteria are usually written after launch
Recurring licenses hide in departmental budgets
success_criteria: The pilot has a clear cost cap
The report links usage to one operational decision

open_questions: Would teams trust a weekly pulse check if follow-up is visible?
Which artifact proves enough value for a capped pilot?
Where would frontline managers abandon the first-run flow?

assets.title: Prototype screen: intro
assets.notes: First screen used in the artifact walkthrough.

assets.title: Prototype screen: action owner
assets.notes: Second screen used in the artifact walkthrough.

assets.title: Field note: shift handover
assets.notes: Input evidence attached to the project.

steps.caption: Explain anonymity and the weekly rhythm

councils.prompt: Would this team trust a weekly TeamPulse check if it shows visible follow-up?
questions: What would make this feel useful rather than like surveillance?
councils.selection_reason: The panel spans sponsor, trust owner, daily user, and budget gatekeeper.
councils.summary: The panel supports a capped pilot only if anonymity, owner visibility, and stop/go criteria are explicit.
councils.exec_summary: Trust is possible, but only when the first screen explains anonymity and the report shows named follow-up owners without exposing individual respondents.

statements.text: I would pilot it if every pulse item ends with an owner and a date; otherwise it becomes another sentiment chart.
The anonymity threshold has to be visible before anyone answers. Trust is built before the first question.
My team will not answer if they think I can identify them. Show what changes next week, not a dashboard.
I need a capped pilot and one operational metric. Participation alone is not a business case.

hypotheses.text: If the first-run flow explains anonymity and shows a named follow-up owner, at least 60% of pilot respondents will support trying TeamPulse for four weeks.

source.text: 2 of 3 imported showcase responses selected support or conditional.

hypothesis_results.note: The small sample validates the onboarding demonstration bet, not a real market claim.

syntheses.title: Showcase report — from question to decision
syntheses.start_input: Synthesize the trust council into an onboarding-ready project report.

payload.arc_narrative: The council turned a broad trust question into concrete proof needs: visible follow-up, clear anonymity, and a capped pilot.
payload.gesamtbild: The product story is not 'measure engagement'; it is 'close one visible loop per week without exposing individuals.'
payload.positionierung: Position TeamPulse as a follow-up accountability layer for hybrid operations teams.

findings.text: Visible follow-up is the adoption hinge.
Keep the pilot capped and reversible.
Frontline managers need phone-first closure, not another dashboard.

citations.quote: Show what changes next week, not a dashboard.

statements.text: Show me the action owner and I might use it.

refs.quote: Show what changes next week, not a dashboard.

surveys.title: TeamPulse pilot readiness survey
surveys.intro: Three questions to validate the pilot risk before launch.

questions.text: What would make you answer a weekly pulse check?
I would support a four-week TeamPulse pilot.

answers.value: If my team thinks answers can be traced back to them.

concept.title: TeamPulse first-run prototype
concept.summary: A two-screen prototype showing privacy and follow-up ownership.

elements.label: Your answer is grouped with the team. Managers see themes, not individual replies.
This week's top issue gets an owner and due date before Friday.

steps.monologue: This has to be the first thing my team sees.
An owner and due date is useful. That is different from just collecting complaints.

outcome.summary: Continued through the flow because privacy and follow-up were both visible.

predicted_behaviors.action: tries the four-week pilot with one team

decisions.title: Run a capped TeamPulse pilot
decisions.decision: Run a four-week pilot at one site, gated by visible owner follow-up and works-council privacy review.

rejected.note: Rejected a broad dashboard rollout because the panel asked for a smaller trust-building pilot.

notes.title: Privacy before value
notes.text: The first screen must explain anonymity before asking for feedback.

notes.title: Owner loop
notes.text: Every result view should name one follow-up owner and due date.

sections.title: Tour path
sections.note: The artifacts the onboarding tour walks through.
